key_points: Atoms are indivisible in chemical reactions and retain their identity
Molecules are formed when two or more atoms combine chemically
Elements consist of only one kind of atom; compounds consist of two or more elements in fixed proportion
Law of multiple proportions: When two elements form more than one compound, the masses of one element combining with fixed mass of the other are in ratio of small whole numbers
Chemical formula shows number and type of atoms in a molecule
Molecular mass and formula mass can be calculated using atomic masses
examples: CO and CO₂ are different compounds formed by carbon and oxygen; their mass ratios follow the law of multiple proportions
Water (H₂O) and hydrogen peroxide (H₂O₂) are compounds of hydrogen and oxygen
Oxygen molecule O₂ contains two atoms; ozone O₃ contains three atoms
Ammonia (NH₃) contains 1 nitrogen and 3 hydrogen atoms per molecule
formulas: Molecular mass = Sum of atomic masses of all atoms in the molecule
Percentage of element = (Mass of element in molecule / Molecular mass) × 100
Mass ratio in compounds can be calculated using atomic masses and chemical formula
exam_tips: Apply law of multiple proportions to identify ratios in compounds
Calculate molecular mass and percentage composition of elements
Distinguish between atoms and molecules using examples
Remember chemical formulas of common compounds
Practice numerical problems involving mass ratios and molecular masses
